mnemonics.title: PIMS-SP Method
mnemonics.content: PIMS-SP reminds you of the 6 main IPC mechanisms: Pipes, Message Queues, Memory Mapping, Shared Memory, Semaphores, and Sockets. Think: 'PImes-SP' sounds like 'Pimes Space' - processes sharing space!

mnemonics.title: SCUBA for Shared Memory
mnemonics.content: SCUBA - 'System V Create/Attach, Use, Bind, Address': Remember divers (SCUBA) go to shared 'spaces' (shared memory) just like processes attach to shared memory segments!

mnemonics.title: MAMA for Message Queues
mnemonics.content: MAMA - Messages Are Moved Asynchronously: Messages in a queue can be sent/received asynchronously without waiting for immediate response, just like leaving a message!

mnemonics.title: P-PULL for Pipe Purpose
mnemonics.content: PULL - 'Processes Unidirectional Line': Pipes are unidirectional (one-way communication), so remember 'PULL' - data PULLed from one end to another!

mnemonics.title: DAD for Deadlock Prevention
mnemonics.content: DAD - 'Denial Avoidance Dynamics': Four conditions needed for deadlock (Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait). Remember 'DAD' helps you think of ways to break these conditions!

mnemonics.title: BIPV for Critical Section
mnemonics.content: BIPV - 'Beware In Process Variable': Three requirements for critical section solution (Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting). Remember 'BIPV' to avoid race conditions!

acronyms.usage: Use when referring to any mechanism that allows processes to communicate and synchronize their actions
Use when referring to standardized API for Unix-like systems; includes IPC mechanisms like pipes, semaphores, shared memory
Named pipes (FIFOs) in Unix follow this order; use to remember that data in pipes maintains order
Used in System V semaphores to automatically undo operation if process terminates; crucial for avoiding deadlocks
System V message queue function to get/create queue; part of msgget(), msgsnd(), msgrcv() family
System V shared memory function to attach process to shared memory segment; remember shmat(), shmdt(), shmget()
Dutch terms for semaphore wait (P) and signal (V) operations; P decrements, V increments the semaphore value

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a large whiteboard (shared memory segment) hung in a common area. Multiple processes can walk up to it, read what's written, and write new information. No messenger needed - they directly access the same whiteboard. The key is they need to take turns or use markers (semaphores) to avoid writing over each other's work!

Think of a post office counter with numbered queue tickets. Processes drop letters (messages) into designated pigeonholes (queues). Other processes pick them up when available. Messages wait in line until retrieved - like waiting for your number to be called!

Picture a garden hose. Water (data) flows in one direction from one end to the other. You cannot send water back through the same hose - you need another pipe for bidirectional communication. The pipe has a limited buffer (water pressure capacity)!

Imagine a railway station with multiple platforms (resource slots). The semaphore is like a digital display showing available platforms. Before a train (process) can use a platform, it checks the display. If platforms are available, it decrements the count and proceeds. After leaving, it increments the count to show a platform is free!

Think of telephone sockets on walls. Just as phones connect through standardized socket interfaces, processes connect through network sockets. Each socket has an address (IP:Port) - like a phone number - that other processes dial to communicate, locally or over a network!

Imagine a single bathroom stall in an office (critical section). Only one person can use it at a time. A queue forms outside, and when the person finishes, they 'signal' the next person. The key rules: only one enters, if free must enter immediately, and waiting time must be bounded!

analogies.analogy: Message Passing is like sending letters through postal mail - you write a letter (create message), send it via post office (kernel), and the recipient receives it later. Shared Memory is like two people sharing the same notebook - they both can read and write to the same pages directly without mailing anything!

Think of a movie theater with a limited number of seats. A semaphore represents the ticket counter. Before entering (critical section), you must get a ticket (P operation). If tickets are available, you take one and enter. When you leave, you return the ticket (V operation), making it available for others!

A named pipe is like a letterbox on a street corner. Anyone who knows the address (pipe name) can drop letters (data) into it. The recipient checks the letterbox periodically. Unlike anonymous pipes which are like temporary walkie-talkies, the letterbox exists permanently until removed!

Imagine two people in a narrow hallway each holding one item and wanting the other's item. Person A has the book and wants the pen (held by B), Person B has the pen and wants the book (held by A). Neither will give up what they have, so they stand forever waiting - classic deadlock!

Synchronization is like traffic signals at an intersection. Without signals, cars (processes) would crash into each other trying to pass through. The traffic light ensures only one direction flows at a time, preventing chaos - exactly like semaphores prevent race conditions!

Signals are like emergency alarms or phone ringtones. They're asynchronous notifications - you don't know exactly when they'll come. When a process receives a signal (like SIGKILL or SIGTERM), it must stop what it's doing and handle it immediately, similar to how you answer a ringing phone!
